text: According to the ideal gas law, at STP (0°C = 273.15 K, 1 atm), 1 mole of ideal gas occupies:
explanation: PV = nRT → V = nRT/P = (1 mol)(0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)(273.15 K)/(1 atm) = 22.4 L. This is the molar volume at STP. Note: at SATP (25°C, 1 bar), molar volume ≈ 24.8 L. MCAT uses 22.4 L/mol at STP (0°C, 1 atm).

text: The pressure of an ideal gas in a rigid container is doubled. Which changes? (Assume constant n and T is maintained by a thermostat)
options: Temperature remains constant as specified; this requires the volume to halve if T is constant
explanation: In a rigid container at constant T: V is fixed. By PV = nRT, P ∝ n (at constant V and T). To double P in a rigid container at constant T: you would need to add more gas (double n). However, if the question implies constant n and T with volume change: V halves (Boyle's Law). The problem states rigid container and thermostat, implying n doubles.

text: Which statement accurately describes the enthalpy of formation (ΔHf°) convention?
options: ΔHf° of any pure element in its standard state = some nonzero value
ΔHf° of any pure element in its most stable allotrope at 25°C and 1 bar = 0 by convention
ΔHf° is the same as the enthalpy of combustion
explanation: By convention: ΔHf° of any element in its standard state (most stable allotrope at 25°C, 1 bar) = 0. Standard states: C(graphite), H₂(g), O₂(g), N₂(g), Cl₂(g), Br₂(l), I₂(s), S(rhombic), P₄(white). ΔHf° can be positive (endothermic, e.g., NO) or negative (exothermic, e.g., H₂O).

text: A student measures the heat of neutralization by mixing 50 mL of 1.0 M HCl and 50 mL of 1.0 M NaOH in a coffee-cup calorimeter. The temperature rises from 22°C to 29°C. Assuming density = 1.0 g/mL and specific heat = 4.18 J/g°C, the heat released per mole of H₂O formed is:
explanation: Total mass = 100 g. q = mcΔT = (100)(4.18)(7) = 2926 J = 2.926 kJ. Moles H₂O = (0.050 L)(1.0 mol/L) = 0.05 mol. Heat per mole = 2.926/0.05 = 58.5 kJ/mol; q = −58.5 kJ/mol (exothermic). Literature value for strong acid/base neutralization: −57.1 kJ/mol. This matches well given approximations.

text: Radioactive iodine-131 (t₁/₂ = 8 days) is used to treat thyroid cancer. After 24 days, what fraction of the original dose remains?
explanation: Number of half-lives = 24/8 = 3. Fraction remaining = (1/2)³ = 1/8. After 24 days, 12.5% of ¹³¹I remains. This is used clinically: thyroid tissue avidly takes up iodine; ¹³¹I delivers β radiation to destroy thyroid cells. After 10 half-lives (80 days), only 1/1024 remains.

text: The Clausius-Clapeyron equation relates:
options: Vapor pressure and boiling point of a liquid
Vapor pressure of a liquid to temperature: ln(P₂/P₁) = −ΔHvap/R × (1/T₂ − 1/T₁)
explanation: Clausius-Clapeyron: ln(P₂/P₁) = −ΔHvap/R × (1/T₂ − 1/T₁). As T increases, vapor pressure increases. Used to predict boiling points at different pressures (e.g., water boils at <100°C at altitude due to lower P) and to calculate enthalpy of vaporization from vapor pressure-temperature data.

text: Raoult's Law (P_A = X_A × P°_A) is used for:
options: Calculating osmotic pressure of electrolyte solutions
Calculating the partial vapor pressure of a solvent in an ideal solution based on its mole fraction
Determining the solubility of gases in liquids (Henry's Law applies here)
explanation: Raoult's Law: P_A = X_A × P°_A (partial vapor pressure of solvent A = mole fraction of A × vapor pressure of pure A). This describes ideal solutions. Positive deviations (P > Raoult's): interactions between unlike molecules weaker than like molecules (e.g., ethanol-water). Negative deviations (P < Raoult's): stronger interactions (e.g., acetone-chloroform).

text: Henry's Law (P = kH × C) relates:
options: Partial pressure of a gas to its solubility in a liquid: higher partial pressure → greater dissolved gas concentration
Vapor pressure of a solvent to the mole fraction of solute
Gas pressure to temperature at constant volume
explanation: Henry's Law: P_gas = k_H × [gas]dissolved. More gas pressure above solution → more dissolved gas. Explains: CO₂ dissolved in soda (pressurized); O₂ uptake in blood (proportional to pO₂ in alveoli); nitrogen narcosis in deep diving (high partial pressure → excess N₂ dissolved in blood).

text: The oxidation state of Mn in KMnO₄ is:
explanation: KMnO₄: K = +1, O = −2 (×4 = −8), so Mn + (−8) + 1 = 0 → Mn = +7. KMnO₄ (potassium permanganate) is a strong oxidizing agent (purple in basic/neutral, colorless/brown in acidic after reduction). It oxidizes alkenes (KMnO₄, cold: diols; hot: cleaved to carboxylic acids), alcohols, and many organics.

text: Which of the following has the shortest bond length?
explanation: Bond length decreases as bond order increases: C–C (single, 1.54 Å) > C=C (double, 1.34 Å) > C≡C (triple, 1.20 Å). Triple bonds are shorter and stronger than double bonds, which are shorter and stronger than single bonds. The more electron pairs shared, the closer nuclei can approach.

text: A solution of NaCl in water freezes at −3.72°C instead of 0°C (pure water). Kf (water) = 1.86°C/m. The molality of NaCl is:
explanation: ΔTf = i × Kf × m. For NaCl: i = 2 (dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻). 3.72 = 2 × 1.86 × m → m = 3.72/3.72 = 1.0 m. So 1.0 mol NaCl per kg water gives a freezing point depression of 3.72°C. This is why salt is spread on icy roads.

text: A 5 kg block accelerates from rest to 10 m/s over 2 seconds. The net force applied is:
explanation: a = Δv/Δt = 10/2 = 5 m/s². F = ma = 5 × 5 = 25 N. (Net force = 25 N in the direction of motion; any friction forces would be in addition to this net force.)

text: A 10 kg box sits on a surface with μs = 0.4 and μk = 0.3 (g = 10 m/s²). The minimum force to start it moving is:
explanation: Maximum static friction: fs_max = μs × N = 0.4 × (10 × 10) = 40 N. Any horizontal force less than 40 N will be balanced by static friction. The minimum force to start moving is just above 40 N. Once moving, kinetic friction = μk × N = 0.3 × 100 = 30 N (less than starting force).

text: A ball is dropped from 20 m height (g = 10 m/s²). The time to reach the ground is:
explanation: h = ½gt² → 20 = ½(10)t² → 20 = 5t² → t² = 4 → t = 2 s. Final speed: v = gt = 10 × 2 = 20 m/s (or v² = 2gh = 2×10×20 = 400 → v = 20 m/s). ✓

text: The slope of a velocity-time graph represents:
explanation: On a velocity-time (v-t) graph, the slope = Δv/Δt = acceleration. Area under the curve = displacement. For constant acceleration (straight line on v-t), slope = a = constant. For uniform motion (horizontal line), slope = 0 = zero acceleration.

text: An object is launched at 45° above horizontal at speed v₀. Its maximum range (horizontal distance) is:
explanation: Range R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g. Maximum range when sin(2θ) = 1 (θ = 45°): R_max = v₀²/g. At any angle θ, complementary angles (e.g., 30° and 60°) give equal range. Maximum height: H = v₀²sin²θ/(2g) = v₀²/(4g) at 45°.

text: Two objects of masses m and 4m are connected by a string over a frictionless pulley (one hangs freely; the other is on a frictionless horizontal surface). The acceleration is:
explanation: Hanging mass (4m) drives system; 4mg − T = 4m·a. Mass on surface: T = m·a. Adding: 4mg = 5m·a → a = 4g/5. Wait: hanging mass drives it: net force = 4mg (weight of hanging mass, if 4m hangs). a = 4mg/(m + 4m) = 4g/5. If m hangs and 4m is on surface: a = mg/5m = g/5.

text: A car moving at 20 m/s applies brakes with a = −5 m/s². The stopping distance is:
explanation: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx → 0 = (20)² + 2(−5)Δx → 0 = 400 − 10Δx → Δx = 40 m. Alternatively: t_stop = v₀/|a| = 20/5 = 4 s; Δx = v₀t + ½at² = 20(4) + ½(−5)(16) = 80 − 40 = 40 m. ✓

text: A roller coaster starts from rest at height h₁ = 30 m and reaches height h₂ = 10 m (g = 10 m/s²). Ignoring friction, its speed at h₂ is:
explanation: Energy conservation: mgh₁ = ½mv² + mgh₂ → v² = 2g(h₁−h₂) = 2(10)(30−10) = 400 → v = 20 m/s. The change in height of 20 m is what matters. (Note: 14.1 m/s would correspond to 10 m drop from rest; 10 m/s to 5 m drop.)

text: Which quantity is conserved in all collisions (elastic and inelastic)?
explanation: Momentum is always conserved in collisions (isolated system, no external forces) — it follows directly from Newton's 3rd Law. Kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions. Total mechanical energy is not conserved if friction or inelastic deformation occurs.

text: The torque produced by a force F acting at distance r from the pivot at angle θ is:
explanation: Torque τ = r × F = rF sinθ (where θ is angle between r and F vectors). Maximum torque when F is perpendicular to r (θ = 90°): τ = rF. Zero torque when F is parallel to r. Units: N·m. For static equilibrium: Στ = 0 and ΣF = 0.

text: For a solid cylinder rolling down an incline, compared to a sphere rolling down the same incline:
options: The cylinder reaches the bottom faster
The sphere reaches the bottom faster (less rotational inertia)
explanation: For a sphere (I = 2/5 mr²), acceleration a_sphere = g sinθ/(1 + 2/5) = 5gsinθ/7. For cylinder (I = ½ mr²), a_cylinder = g sinθ/(1 + 1/2) = 2gsinθ/3. Since 5/7 > 2/3, the sphere accelerates faster and reaches the bottom first. Hollow cylinder has I = mr² (slowest).

text: A pump lifts water at flow rate Q = 0.02 m³/s through height h = 5 m (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). The minimum power of the pump is:
explanation: Power = rate of doing work against gravity = ρQgh = (1000)(0.02)(10)(5) = 1000 W = 1 kW. P = mgh/t = (ρ × Q × t × g × h)/t = ρQgh. This is the minimum ideal power (no friction losses in real pumps).

text: The rate of heat conduction through a wall is greatest when:
options: Wall thickness is large and ΔT is small
Wall thickness is small, thermal conductivity is high, and ΔT is large
Area is small and ΔT is large
Temperature is uniform throughout the wall
explanation: Fourier's Law: Q/t = kA(ΔT/L). To maximize heat flow: high k (thermal conductivity), large A (cross-section), large ΔT (temperature difference), small L (thin wall). Insulation works by having small k (e.g., fiberglass, air gaps) and increased effective L.

text: The first law of thermodynamics, ΔU = q + w, where w = −PΔV (work done BY the system). For an adiabatic process:
explanation: Adiabatic: no heat exchange with surroundings (q = 0). By first law: ΔU = 0 + w = w. For expansion against external pressure: w = −PΔV (work done by system is negative for expansion if convention w = work done ON system). Adiabatic expansion cools the gas (ΔU < 0, T decreases).
